query: levetiracetam vs sodium valproate for focal seizures

retrieved_docs.title: Absence Seizure -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The first-line treatment for absence epilepsy is ethosuximide. A randomized controlled trial in 2010 that included 446 children with CAE showed that ethosuximide and valproic acid were superior to lamotrigine. [17] However, this study had a low seizure-free rate, with 53% of patients in the ethosuximide group, 58% in the valproic acid group, and 29% in patients taking lamotrigine. The group that received valproic acid had significantly lower scores on attentional measures compared to the ethosuximide and lamotrigine groups. For this reason, ethosuximide is the preferred agent for treating absence epilepsy. A study showed that only one-quarter of children with absence epilepsy became seizure-free with levetiracetam. If effective, levetiracetam can control absence epilepsy at a relatively low dose (usually <40 mg/kg/d). Increasing doses are not effective if levetiracetam is not initially effective. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Seizure Medications -- Administration -- Initiation of Antiepileptic Drugs
retrieved_docs.content: One large randomized trial, the Standard and New Antiepileptic Drugs (SANAD) trial, demonstrated some comparative advantages of certain AEDs when treating focal or generalized epilepsy. In the end, when comparing valproate, lamotrigine, or topiramate for generalized seizures, they recommended valproic acid as their first-line choice. Additionally, when comparing carbamazepine, gabapentin, lamotrigine, oxcarbazepine, and topiramate for focal seizures, lamotrigine was cited as the first-line choice. Of note, regarding partial onset seizures specifically, perampanel, lacosamide, brivaracetam, and eslicarbazepine acetate are a few of the recently FDA-approved seizure medications for monotherapy.

retrieved_docs.title: New Onset Refractory Status Epilepticus -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with benzodiazepine refractory SE, a second-line antiepileptic agent should be attempted immediately at appropriate doses. In a randomized control trial, IV levetiracetam, IV fosphenytoin, and IV sodium valproate were shown to have similar safety and efficacy in established SE patients. [34] The effectiveness of each subsequent antiepileptic added in aborting status epilepticus decreases after initiating the first antiepileptic agent

retrieved_docs.title: Rett Syndrome -- Treatment / Management -- Seizure disorders
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 60% of patients with RTT have some form of seizure disorder. Treatment options to alleviate seizures include valproate, lamotrigine, levetiracetam, carbamazepine, and AEDs. A multicenter study described that generalized tonic-clonic and myoclonic epilepsy were the most typical seizure semiologies, whereas absence and focal epilepsy were less prevalent. Antiseizure medications significantly alter seizure characteristics. The most commonly used antiseizure drugs affecting the severity and frequency of seizures are valproate, levetiracetam, lamotrigine, and clobazam. Reduction of seizures and improvement of cognitive functions were also observed in the ketogenic diet, vagal nerve stimulation, and steroid treatment. [47]

retrieved_docs.title: Status Epilepticus -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Antiepileptic drugs should be administered concurrently with benzodiazepines. Choices include fosphenytoin (20 mg/kg phenytoin equivalents [PE] up to 100 to 150 PE/minute, phenytoin (20 mg/kg up to 25 to 50 mg/minute), levetiracetam (40 to 60 mg/kg up to a total of 4500 mg over 15 minutes) and valproic acid (30mg/kg at up to 10 mg/kg/minute) (concern for hepatotoxicity with valproic acid use in children younger than two years).

retrieved_docs.title: Levetiracetam -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Seizure -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: If deciding to start drug therapy, many medications are options to treat a chronic seizure disorder or epilepsy as first-line medication or adjunctive medications. Selection may be guided by side effects and in consultation with a neurologist. They can be grouped based on their mechanism of action and include sodium channel blockers (carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, eslicarbazepine, phenytoin, fosphenytoin, lamotrigine, lacosamide, and zonisamide), and agonists of GABA receptor (benzodiazepine and barbiturates). Other drugs with associated mechanisms include GABA reuptake inhibitors (tiagabine), inhibitors of GABA-transaminase (vigabatrin), glutamate antagonists (topiramate, felbamate, perampanel), medications with binding to synaptic vesicle 2A protein (levetiracetam, brivaracetam), and drugs with multiple mechanisms (gabapentin, pregabalin, valproic acid).

retrieved_docs.title: Seizure -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The best second-line medication is unclear even after completing a highly anticipated randomized trial of benzodiazepine refractory status epilepticus- the established status epilepticus treatment trial (ESETT). Second-line medications include fosphenytoin, valproate, levetiracetam, and others. Doses used in the ESETT study are listed below, given with an infusion time of ten minutes. The dosing of these medications in this study was higher than doses typically used in clinical practice. Clinicians have noted similar incidences of adverse effects with these medications, and no one drug was superior to the others. [20] Fosphenytoin 20PE/kg (up to 1500 phenytoin equivalents) Valproate 40mg/kg (up to 300 mg) Levetiracetam 60/mg (up to 4500 mg)

retrieved_docs.title: Myoclonic Epilepsy of Infancy -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Other forms of myoclonic epilepsy discussed under differential diagnosis are challenging to treat. The primary treatment modalities for West syndrome are ACTH/corticosteroids or vigabatrin. Clobazam, sodium valproate, and zonisamide may be second-line drugs. First-line medications for Dravet syndrome are a combination of antiseizure drugs (eg, clobazam and valproic acid); alternative treatment includes stiripentol, topiramate, fenfluramine, ketogenic diet, or vagus nerve stimulation. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Myoclonus -- Treatment / Management -- Levetiracetam
retrieved_docs.content: A relatively "clean" AED, with renal rather than hepatic clearance and a comparatively mild side effect profile, levetiracetam [62] is frequently the first-line therapy in cortical myoclonus. Its antimyoclonic effect is theorized to arise from its binding to the synaptic vesicle protein 2A (SV2A), although the precise mechanism of action has not yet been established. Standard dosing regimens fall between 1000 and 3000 mg daily, divided into two doses. Side effects include headache, dizziness, and fatigue. Behavioral side effects sometimes limit its usage, but these are more common in the treatment of epilepsy(3-4%) than myoclonus. [62]

retrieved_docs.title: Seizure Medications -- Administration -- Initiation of Antiepileptic Drugs
retrieved_docs.content: AEDs can be broken down into two categories: broad-spectrum and narrow-spectrum. Broad-spectrum AEDs treat a wide variety of seizure types, as the name suggests, and are a good initial choice, especially when the classification of seizure type is uncertain. These AEDs include but are not limited to levetiracetam, lamotrigine, zonisamide, topiramate, valproic acid, clonazepam, perampanel, clobazam, and rufinamide.

retrieved_docs.title: Juvenile Myoclonic Epilepsy -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: JME is relatively easy to control with anti-epilepsy drugs (AEDs), and most patients respond to monotherapy. Valproic acid is the most effective medication and drug of choice for treatment of JME. [21] It is a broad-spectrum medication and treats all seizure types of JME. Its use requires a great deal of caution in women of childbearing age, given its well known teratogenic potential. [22] Other treatment options include levetiracetam, lamotrigine, topiramate, and zonisamide. [23] [24] [25] [26] [27] Lamotrigine can lead to a worsening in myoclonic seizures but is still widely used for the treatment of JME as it controls the other seizure types. [23] [28] If only absence seizures are present, ethosuximide can be a therapy choice. Clonazepam is effective against myoclonic jerks. Contraindicated agents include carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, phenytoin (sodium channel blocking agents), given their potential to exacerbate myoclonic and absence seizures. But, they can be useful for the treatment of GTC seizures in JME. [29] Other medications to avoid include vigabatrin, tiagabine, gabapentin, pregabalin, and primidone. [30] JME is intractable in only a few patients, and combination therapy with or without vagus nerve stimulation (VNS) is an option in such cases. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Focal Impaired Awareness Seizure -- Treatment / Management -- Medications
retrieved_docs.content: Except for ethosuximide, all other currently available AEDs can be used to treat focal impaired awareness seizures. The choice of medication depends on the patient's preference, comorbid conditions, drug interactions, and adverse effect profile; monotherapy is preferred initially. An increased dose of a single agent may be required to achieve seizure control before adding another agent. Classical AEDs used for focal impaired awareness seizures include carbamazepine, valproate, phenytoin, and phenobarbital. Newer agents available are levetiracetam, topiramate, lamotrigine, gabapentin, oxcarbazepine, zonisamide, felbamate, tiagabine, pregabalin, and lacosamide. The most common first-line drugs are carbamazepine, phenytoin, valproic acid, and oxcarbazepine.

retrieved_docs.title: Temporal Seizure -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Upon diagnosis of temporal lobe epilepsy, initial management should be pharmacological intervention with one of a variety of AEDs. Older AEDs such as phenytoin, valproate, carbamazepine, and phenobarbital, are equal in efficacy to newer AEDs like lamotrigine, gabapentin, or levetiracetam , but correlate with a higher rate of adverse effects such as hepatotoxicity. Clinicians should avoid valproate and topiramate if possible, and lamotrigine and may consider levetiracetam, due to clinical evidence of higher rates of favorable outcomes in pregnancy. [23] [24] Women of childbearing age should also understand the potential teratogenicity with the use of AEDs in the first trimester .

retrieved_docs.title: Levetiracetam -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: It is chemically unrelated to other antiepileptic drugs. However, its favorable safety profile, distinct mechanism of action, and fewer drug interactions make it an attractive therapeutic choice for treating seizures.

retrieved_docs.title: New Onset Refractory Status Epilepticus -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: If seizures continue despite treatment with a second-line agent, another anti-epileptic agent should be used before progressing to IV anesthetic agents. IV phenytoin/fosphenytoin, phenobarbital, valproate, levetiracetam, and lacosamide; have been noted to be effective in the treatment of established SE with no preference over each other. [32] Hence one of these agents, either as single or in combination, can be used for treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2456
retrieved_docs.content: Epilepsy (الصرع) is a neurological disorder that can present in various forms, including benign neonatal epilepsy (epilepsie benigní neonatální), which is classified as a subtype of epilepsy. A broad spectrum of antiepileptic medications is recognized as potential treatments for epilepsy. Valproic acid derivatives such as valproát vápenatý, Valproat, and kyselina valproová, as well as the related compound felbamát, are commonly employed to control seizures. Additional agents with efficacy in epilepsy include gabapentin enacarbil, lamotrigin, levetiracetam, mefenytoin, phenytoin sodium, tiagabin, trimethadion, and zonisamid. Each of these drugs may be used to reduce seizure frequency and improve patient outcomes across the spectrum of epileptic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1423
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic class of antiepileptic agents, identified as Anti‑epileptic Agent [EPC], comprises a broad spectrum of medications used to prevent and control seizures. This class, referred to as antiepileptics, includes the organic acids and salts kyselina valproová, valproát sodný, valproát vápenatý, and magnesium valproate, all of which are variants of valproic acid. Additional members are zonisamid, ethosuximid, gabapentin enacarbil, lakosamid, levetiracetam, phenytoin sodium, tiagabin hydrochlorid, and topiramat, which is listed as a child of the antiepileptics class. The grouping “Other antiepileptics in ATC” is defined as a subclass that is an instance of antiepileptics. Together, these agents constitute the antiepileptic therapeutic class, providing clinicians with a diverse set of options for effective seizure management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2493
retrieved_docs.content: Mefobarbital may be used to treat generalized onset seizures, offering therapeutic benefit in controlling these seizure episodes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 442
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic class “Anti‑epileptic Agent [EPC]” (anticonvulsants) comprises a broad spectrum of individual drugs that are indicated for the treatment of epilepsy (الصرع) and its subtypes, including benign neonatal epilepsy. Representative agents within this class include phenytoin sodium, primidon, tiagabin (both as base and hydrochloride), topiramat (topiramate), trimethadion, lamotrigin, tiagabin, ethotoin, ethosuximid, felbamát, fosphenytoin (including fosphenytoin sodium), gabapentin enacarbil, mefenytoin, valproát sodný (sodium valproate), valproát vápenatý (calcium valproate), magnesium valproate, vigabatrin, zonisamid, lakosamid, levetiracetam, methsuximide, and oxkarbazepin. All of these agents share the common therapeutic goal of controlling seizure activity and improving patient outcomes across the various forms of epilepsy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1057
retrieved_docs.content: The seizure type نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة is classified within the broader category of epilepsy; convulsions, indicating that it represents a generalized onset non‑motor seizure. In clinical practice, the barbiturate mefobarbital may be used to treat نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة, providing therapeutic benefit for patients who experience this specific form of seizure. Recognizing نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة as part of epilepsy; convulsions informs appropriate selection of mefobarbital in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1293
retrieved_docs.content: Antiepileptics represent a broad therapeutic category that includes numerous individual agents and sub‑classes. Within this category, tiagabin is identified as a child drug of the antiepileptic group. Several compounds are specifically noted as therapeutic classes of antiepileptics, including ethotoin, mefenytoin, zonisamid, ethosuximid, felbamát, fosphenytoin sodium, lakosamid, levetiracetam, magnesium valproate, methsuximide, and trimethadion. Together, these agents illustrate the diverse pharmacologic options available for seizure control, highlighting the importance of selecting appropriate antiepileptic drugs based on their distinct therapeutic class properties to optimize patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 964
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑epileptic Agent [EPC] belongs to the therapeutic class of antiepileptics, a broad group of drugs indicated for the treatment of epilepsy (الصرع) and its various phenotypes, including generalized, focal and benign neonatal epilepsy. The class encompasses numerous individual agents, each with distinct chemical forms or derivatives. Valproat serves as a parent compound that gives rise to several related formulations such as valproát sodný, valproát vápenatý, magnesium valproate and other valproate salts. Within the same class, lamotrigin and tiagabin (including tiagabin hydrochloride) are identified as child agents derived from the broader antiepileptic category. Additional antiepileptic medications include phenytoin sodium, primidon, trimethadion, ethotoin, mefenytoin, vigabatrin, ethosuximid, felbamát, fosphenytoin, fosphenytoin sodium, gabapentin enacarbil, lakosamid, levetiracetam, methsuximide and oxkarbazepin. All of these agents share the common mechanism of reducing seizure activity and are employed to control seizures across different age groups and epilepsy subtypes, thereby improving patient outcomes in epilepsy care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2568
retrieved_docs.content: Epilepsy (الصرع) is a chronic neurological disorder characterized by recurrent seizures, and neonatal benign epilepsy (epilepsie benigní neonatální) is recognized as a specific subtype that falls under the broader category of epilepsy. A wide range of anti‑epileptic agents are indicated for the treatment of epilepsy, including the generic class Anti‑epileptic Agent [EPC] and individual drugs such as Valproat (valproic acid), ethosuximid (ethosuximide), felbamát (felbamate), fosphenytoin, kyselina valproová (valproic acid), magnesium valproate, methsuximide, phenytoin sodium, tiagabin hydrochlorid (tiagabine hydrochloride), topiramat (topiramate), vigabatrin, and zonisamid (zonisamide). Each of these medications employs distinct mechanisms—such as sodium‑channel blockade, enhancement of GABAergic inhibition, or modulation of calcium‑channel activity—to suppress abnormal neuronal firing and control seizure activity. The choice of a particular agent depends on factors such as seizure type, patient age, comorbid conditions, and potential side‑effect profiles, making individualized therapy essential for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2143
retrieved_docs.content: Epilepsy (الصرع) is a neurological disorder that can be managed with a variety of antiepileptic medications. Several formulations of valproate are used in treatment, including calcium valproate (valproát vápenatý), magnesium valproate, and valproic acid (kyselina valproová). Other effective agents comprise ethosuximide (ethosuximid), felbamate (felbamát), fosphenytoin, lamotrigine (lamotrigin), oxcarbazepine (oxkarbazepin), phenytoin sodium, and vigabatrin. Each of these drugs may be prescribed to control seizures associated with epilepsy. In addition, benign neonatal epilepsy (epilepsie benigní neonatální) is recognized as a specific subtype of epilepsy, sharing the underlying pathophysiology of the broader disorder while presenting with distinct clinical features in newborns. Effective management of epilepsy therefore involves selecting appropriate antiepileptic agents based on the type and severity of the condition, including its neonatal forms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 219
retrieved_docs.content: Antiepileptic agents form a distinct therapeutic class within central‑nervous‑system‑active drugs and include hydantoin‑derived anticonvulsants (phenytoin, fosphenytoin, fosphenytoin sodium, phenytoin sodium, mefenytoin, mefobarbital, Dihydan, chlordantoin, nitrofurantoin), valproate compounds (valproic acid, sodium valproate, calcium valproate, magnesium valproate, valproate sodium, other valproate salts), gabapentin and its prodrug gabapentin enacarbil, lacosamide, lamotrigine, levetiracetam, tiagabine (tiagabine hydrochloride), ethotoin, felbamate, vigabatrin, zonisamide, ethosuximide, methsuximide, trimethadion, oxcarbazepine, primidone, and topiramate. These drugs share the primary indication of treating epilepsy (الصرع) and are organized as “children” of the antiepileptic therapeutic class. Mechanistically they span sodium‑channel blockers (phenytoin, fosphenytoin, oxcarbazepine, lamotrigine, lacosamide), calcium‑channel modulators (ethosuximide, methsuximide), GABA‑enhancing

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Epilepsy (الصرع) is a neurological disorder characterized by recurrent seizures, and benign neonatal epilepsy (epilepsie benigní neonatální) is recognized as a specific subtype that falls under the broader category of epilepsy. Several antiepileptic drugs have been identified as potential treatments for epilepsy. Calcium valproate (valproát vápenatý) may treat epilepsy, as may ethosuximid, ethotoin, felbamát, fosphenytoin sodium, gabapentin enacarbil, levetiracetam, oxkarbazepin, phenytoin sodium, and zonisamid. These medications, each with distinct mechanisms of action, are employed to reduce neuronal excitability and control seizure activity across various epilepsy syndromes, including benign neonatal epilepsy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 934
retrieved_docs.content: Antiepileptic agents (EPC) constitute a therapeutic class indicated for the treatment of epilepsy (الصرع) and its subtypes, including benign neonatal epilepsy. Within this class, the ATC hierarchy defines a parent–child (“isa”) relationship linking “Other antiepileptics in ATC” to the broader antiepileptics category. Major child entities include Valproat and its various salts (valproic acid, sodium valproate, calcium valproate, magnesium valproate, sodium salt of valproic acid, valproate sodium), as well as lamotrigine, tiagabin (and tiagabin hydrochloride), levetiracetam, ethosuximide, methsuximide, oxcarbazepine, phenytoin sodium, primidon, trimethadion, mefenytoin, felbamate, fosphenytoin (including fosphenytoin sodium), gabapentin enacarbil, vigabatrin, zonisamide, lakosamide, ethotoin, ethoxuximide, and other agents listed under “Other antiepileptics in ATC.” These drugs span several pharmacologic mechanisms—sodium‑channel blockade (e.g., phenytoin, oxcarbazepine), calcium‑channel modulation (e.g., ethosuximide, methsuximide), GABA‑enhancement (e.g., vigabatrin, tiagabin), and broad‑spectrum anticonvulsant activity (e.g., valproate derivatives, lamotrigine, levetiracetam)—providing clinicians with diverse options to achieve seizure control across generalized and focal seizure disorders. The extensive classification underscores the importance of selecting appropriate antiepileptic agents tailored to the specific seizure type and patient characteristics for optimal therapeutic outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1028
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic class “Antiepileptic Agent [EPC]” comprises a broad group of drugs indicated for the treatment of epilepsy (الصرع) and its subtypes, including benign neonatal epilepsy. Members of this class include lamotrigin, tiagabin (both as tiagabin and tiagabin hydrochloride), primidon, trimethadion, vigabatrin, ethotoin, mefenytoin, valproát vápenatý, magnesium valproate, ethosuximid, felbamát, fosphenytoin (including fosphenytoin sodium), gabapentin enacarbil, lakosamid, levetiracetam, methsuximide, oxkarbazepin, phenytoin sodium, and the subgroup “Other antiepileptics in ATC.” All of these agents share the common purpose of controlling seizure activity, and their classification within the antiepileptics therapeutic class supports systematic prescribing, pharmacologic understanding, and coordinated patient care across generalized and neonatal forms of epilepsy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2455
retrieved_docs.content: Epilepsy (الصرع) is a chronic neurological disorder that can be managed with a wide range of antiepileptic medications. Valproát vápenatý (calcium valproate) and valproát sodný (sodium valproate) are both formulations of valproate that may be used to treat epilepsy. Other agents that may treat epilepsy include ethosuximid, methsuximide, mefenytoin, phenytoin sodium, oxkarbazepin, lakosamid, zonisamid, vigabatrin, and gabapentin enacarbil. Each of these drugs works through distinct mechanisms to reduce seizure frequency and severity, providing clinicians with multiple therapeutic options tailored to individual patient needs. In addition, benign neonatal epilepsy (epilepsie benigní neonatální) is recognized as a specific subtype that falls under the broader category of epilepsy, highlighting the heterogeneity of seizure disorders and the importance of selecting appropriate treatment strategies for different epilepsy phenotypes. Effective management of epilepsy therefore involves identifying the specific seizure type and employing one or more of these antiepileptic agents to achieve optimal seizure control.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1535
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑epileptic Agent [EPC] is recognized as a therapeutic option for epilepsy (الصرع). A wide range of antiepileptic medications are also indicated for the treatment of epilepsy, including valproát vápenatý (calcium valproate), gabapentin, ethosuximid, felbamát, fosphenytoin, fosphenytoin sodium, lakosamid, levetiracetam, methsuximide, tiagabin hydrochlorid, trimethadion, and valproát sodný (sodium valproate). In addition, the specific condition benign neonatal epilepsy (epilepsie benigní neonatální) is classified as a subtype of epilepsy, indicating that it shares the same underlying disease category and can be managed with the aforementioned antiepileptic agents. Collectively, these agents constitute the pharmacologic armamentarium for controlling seizures across the spectrum of epilepsy, from general forms to specific neonatal presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1501
retrieved_docs.content: Epilepsy (الصرع) is a neurological disorder that can be managed with a variety of anti‑epileptic agents. The generic category “Anti‑epileptic Agent [EPC]” as well as specific drugs such as valproát vápenatý (valproic acid), felbamát (felbamate), levetiracetam, magnesium valproate, mefenytoin (mephenytoin), phenytoin sodium, primidon (primidone), tiagabin hydrochlorid (tiagabine hydrochloride), topiramat (topiramate) and trimethadion are all reported to may treat epilepsy. In addition, benign neonatal epilepsy (epilepsie benigní neonatální) is recognized as a subtype that falls under the broader category of epilepsy. Together, these relationships highlight the range of pharmacologic options available for controlling seizures across different forms of epilepsy.
